title: The Nicene Creed, Part 4
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 4 (Mr. Isaiah Couch).txt
outline: Part 4 of the Nicene Creed series
Exposition of the relevant article(s) of the Nicene Creed
summary: Part 4 of the October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, presented by Mr. Isaiah Couch. Continues the verse-by-verse exposition of the Creed in its historical and theological context.
transcript:
## Introduction

I am once again incredibly humbled and thrilled to be asked by our session to speak this morning as we continue our Sunday school series focusing on the Nicene Creed. As someone who has spent the majority of his life so far outside of the Reformed tradition, I am truly thankful for how this tradition — our tradition — and more specifically our church, during a Reformation series each year, takes the time to focus on how God through the power of his Spirit has been faithful to his people in continuing to reform and transform his bride into the image of her head, our Lord Jesus Christ. There is great comfort in knowing that the church down through the ages has always been *semper reformanda* — always reforming — as we seek to bring every thought captive to the obedience of Christ (2 Corinthians 10:5).

It is always a joy during Reformation month to reflect on the great cloud of witnesses that has gone before us. I have used this quote from Kevin DeYoung in the past because I believe it summarizes well the state of the church today. DeYoung says that the chief theological task now facing the Western church is not to reinvent or to be relevant, but to remember. We must remember the faith once delivered to the saints. We must remember the truths that spark Reformation, revival, and regeneration. And because we want to remember all this, we must also remember — if we are fortunate enough to have ever heard of them in the first place — our creeds, confessions, and catechisms.

So let us continue this morning by remembering, or for some of us perhaps learning for the first time, this historic creed of the church: the Nicene Creed.

## Series Review

Just a brief summary of where we have been. We have seen some of the historical events surrounding this creed, along with the theological controversies that had arisen during the fourth century. Pastor Tito Lyro spoke on how Emperor Constantine called for a church council in AD 325 to discuss a pressing controversy that had erupted in Alexandria, Egypt, involving a man named Arius of Alexandria. Despite what many TikTok theologians might say, the council did not convene to discuss what books of the Bible would be canonized — I hope you have listened to Wesley Hoff address that question in the past.

Rather, the controversy at hand was whether the Son of God is truly God in substance. Is the Son *homoiousios* — of similar substance with the Father — or is he *homoousios* — of the same substance with the Father? One group claimed *homoiousios*, another claimed *homoousios*, and the majority just wanted to get along. In the end — and hopefully some of you will get this reference — *homoousios*, the orthodox view, prevailed, and Arianism was condemned by the council. If you don't get the reference, come talk with me afterward and I'll tell you about a certain Saint Nicholas of Myra at the council. It may be true; it may not be true. But it's fun to talk about.

The document produced by the Council of Nicaea — commonly called the Creed of Nicaea — provided the foundational theological ideas that would later be further developed and solidified in the statement of faith we commonly know as the Nicene Creed in AD 381. I have included the original Creed of Nicaea, and you can see that some parts are missing. The statement on the Holy Spirit is nearly nonexistent, and the section on Christ is much shorter — those would be developed and formulated over the next fifty years.

Last week, Elder Nick Anderson and Elder Scott Hollander spoke on the first two articles of the Creed. As a way to refresh our memory, you can either turn to it in your hymnal or I will put it up on the screen. I would like us to read again that opening section before we begin:

> *"We believe in one God, the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth, and of all things visible and invisible. And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the only begotten Son of God. Begotten of the Father before all worlds. God of God, light of light, very God of very God. Begotten, not made, being of one substance with the Father by whom all things were made."*

## The Focus of Articles 3–7: The Work of Christ

This morning we come to what could be considered articles 3 through 7 of the Nicene Creed. You will notice that this is one of the longest sections we will address in our series. It does not speak directly to the *person* of Christ, but rather to his *work*. This transition from focusing on the person of Christ to his work is seen in the words "who for us and for our salvation."

In the book by DeYoung that Pastor Tito Lyro recommended as our guide, DeYoung says: if "one substance with the Father" is the heart of the Nicene Creed, then the phrase "who for us and for our salvation" is the beating of that very heart. The reason that the church cares — and must care — so much about the person and work of Christ is because of what the doctrine means for us and for our salvation. Only a Savior who is truly God of God, light of light, very God of very God, can save us from our sins. And only a Savior who is also truly and fully man can stand in our place as the true and better Adam (Romans 5).

These truths are affirmed and recognized in the Nicene Creed as core beliefs of what it means to be a Christian. As Pastor Tito Lyro, Elder Nick Anderson, and Elder Scott Hollander have already stated in their lessons, I reaffirm what they have said: if you leave the Creed, you have left the faith, and your soul is in grave danger. These are the very doctrines that Jude says in verse 3 were once delivered to the saints.

So let us now begin to examine this beating heart of the Creed and the work of Christ. The Creed states:

> *"...who for us men and for our salvation came down from heaven and was incarnate by the Holy Spirit of the Virgin Mary and was made man. And was crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate. He suffered and was buried and the third day he rose again according to the scriptures. And ascended into heaven and sits on the right hand of the Father and he shall come again with glory to judge the living and the dead, whose kingdom shall have no end."*

Contained in this section of the Creed is what is commonly known as the doctrine of Christ's **estate of humiliation** and his **estate of exaltation**.

It is important to remember that this doctrine is not something newly formulated in the Nicene Creed or the Council of Nicaea. The Creed of Nicaea, produced by the Council of Nicaea in 325, also contains similar language. Even the earlier creeds — the Old Roman Creed around AD 140, the Apostles' Creed around AD 180 — communicated the same doctrine in almost identical language. But even more important is the reality that Scripture itself communicates this same doctrine.

In Philippians 2:5–11, the Apostle Paul writes:

> *"Let this mind be in you which was also in Christ Jesus, who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even the death of the cross. Therefore God also has highly exalted him and given him the name which is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven, and of those on earth, and of those under the earth, and that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father."* (Philippians 2:5–11)

Here in this passage, we see Christ's estate of humiliation and his estate of exaltation. Nothing new is being said by the Nicene Creed — or by creeds before or after it.

Part of being truly man — and I wanted to emphasize this — is that Christ was born like us, suffered like us, knew hunger and thirst like us, was tempted like us, knew betrayal, and ultimately died like us. The writer of Hebrews reminds and encourages the believer of the great comfort in knowing this truth:

> *"For we do not have a high priest who cannot sympathize with our weaknesses, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin. Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need."* (Hebrews 4:15–16)

There is no estate of exaltation that is not first preceded by an estate of humiliation. Our Lord exemplified this when he prayed in John 17:1–5:

> *"Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son also may glorify you. As you have given him authority over all flesh, that he should give eternal life to as many as you have given him. And this is eternal life, that they may know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent. I have glorified you on the earth. I have finished the work which you have given me to do. And now, Father, glorify me together with yourself, with the glory which I had with you before the world was."* (John 17:1–5)

When we read Isaiah 52 and 53, we see that the sin-bearing servant will be highly exalted:

> *"Behold, my servant shall deal prudently; he shall be exalted and extolled and be very high."* (Isaiah 52:13)

And yet when we continue reading in Isaiah 53, exaltation is not what we first see:

> *"For he shall grow up before him as a tender plant, and as a root out of dry ground. He has no form or comeliness; and when we see him, there is no beauty that we should desire him. He is despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief."* (Isaiah 53:2–3)

So there is no estate of exaltation without first an estate of humiliation. This is why Christ's estate of humiliation is something we should never overlook — and it is certainly not something the Nicene Creed overlooks. The Creed begins by first addressing humiliation and then turns to exaltation.

## The Estate of Humiliation

### Incarnation

> *"And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth."* (John 1:14)

To meditate upon the thought that the Son of God became flesh is — as the English Puritan John Flavel rightly said — an abasement that none can rightly comprehend. For the one who is, as the Nicene Creed says, "the only begotten Son of God, begotten of the Father before all worlds, God of God, light of light, very God of very God" — for him to be made flesh is an act of condescension and humiliation that we can never rightly comprehend.

Our Lord was not born in an estate of earthly wealth and glory, clothed in the finest garments, bathed in the purest waters, and adorned with the splendor of the earth. Neither was he born in a normal estate, in a warm home, a middle-class life with clean clothes. He was born in a low estate — an animal stable, clothed in rough cloth, placed in a feeding trough — adorned, as our catechism says, with "diverse circumstances of more than ordinary abasement."

> *"And she brought forth her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no room for them in the inn."* (Luke 2:7)

This is Christ's state in the first part of his humiliation: his incarnation. I was thinking about this in preparation for this lesson — have we ever thought about the fact that when there was a multitude of heavenly hosts praising God in the presence of lowly shepherds, that moment was still part of his humiliation? Because the one who could rip the sky open to reveal myriad upon myriad of heavenly hosts, the same one who could have commanded every knee to bow and loosened every tongue to proclaim that he is Lord — he lay silent in a stable feeding trough, wrapped in cloth. John Flavel was certainly correct: we cannot rightly comprehend this first part of Christ's humiliation in his incarnation.

### Born Under the Law

Also contained in this same section of the Nicene Creed is the simple phrase "and was made man." This phrase is certainly in reference to the incarnation and being born in the likeness of men, but it also means that Christ's estate of humiliation included his life in being born under the law of God.

> *"But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law, that we might receive the adoption as sons."* (Galatians 4:4–5)

So not only was the Son of God born of a woman of the lowest state in her society and in her world, but he was also born under the subjection of the law of God to redeem those who were under the curse and wrath of God. The life of Christ, in being born under the law of God, is one marked by personal, perfect, and perpetual obedience to the very law he was born under — because that was necessary for us men and for our salvation.

Consider the thought that the Son of God who in the beginning was with God and who was God (John 1:1), through whom all things were made and upheld and purposed to glorify him (Colossians 1:16, 18), took not only flesh but was despised and rejected by men (Isaiah 53:3), came to his own and his own did not recognize him or receive him (John 1:10–11). This is also a humiliation of the one deserving all praise and all glory.

> *"Therefore, in all things he had to be made like his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people. For in that he himself has suffered, being tempted, he is able to aid those who are tempted."* (Hebrews 2:17–18)

Our Lord Jesus Christ in his estate of humiliation experienced hunger, thirst, exhaustion, weariness, sorrow, and suffering in our broken and fallen world — all for us men and for our salvation. When speaking of this part of Christ's estate of humiliation, J. G. Vos once wrote that we should be filled with the deepest gratitude to him who endured such affliction and deprivation for our sake. We should resist the temptation to yield to discouragement and despair when we are faced with troubles and hardships in our earthly pilgrimage, remembering that our Savior, the Lord of Glory, endured much more grievous troubles and hardships because of his great love for us.

### Death and Burial

The Nicene Creed continues to progress through this state by saying that Christ's estate of humiliation was not only in his incarnation, or being made man and born under the law of God, but also, as it says, "crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate. He suffered and was buried."

Here we have the final parts comprising Christ's estate of humiliation. Here we have the lowest steps in Christ's descent. Here we have the words of the prophet Isaiah in Isaiah 53 fulfilled:

> *"He was stricken, smitten by God, wounded for our transgressions, bruised for our iniquities — led as a lamb to the slaughter, cut off from the land of the living, and they made his grave with the wicked."* (Isaiah 53)

I could not help but think of the hymn: *Tell me, who hears him groaning — was there ever grief like his? Friends through fear his cause disowning, foes insulting his distress. Many hands were raised to wound him, none would interpose to save — but the deepest stroke that pierced him was the stroke that justice gave.*

It is Christ who faced the terrors of death and the powers of darkness, who felt and bore the weight of God's wrath, and laid down his life as an offering for sin — by enduring the painful, shameful, and cursed death of the cross.

And while the passion of Christ and his death upon the cross is our everything — it is what we cling to — it is still not yet the deepest and lowest point of his humiliation. It is proper to understand that it was in his being given over in death to the burial of the grave that was the lowest part of his estate.

> *"For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth."* (Matthew 12:40)

By Christ being buried and remaining in the state of the dead and under the power of death for a time, he bore the wages of our sin — for as Romans 6:23 says, "the wages of sin is death." It was not just in his dying on the cross, but also in his remaining in the state of the dead and under the power of death, that our salvation was purchased and our debt was paid. By entering into death and remaining in that place, Christ took upon himself the curse and terror of the grave for his people.

> *"Inasmuch then as the children have partaken of flesh and blood, he himself likewise shared in the same, that through death he might destroy him who had the power of death, that is, the devil, and release those who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage."* (Hebrews 2:14–15)

It is only here — once we have reached this lowest step and understood the deepest depth of Christ's estate of humiliation — that we can truly know the beauty of Christ's estate of exaltation.

## The Estate of Exaltation

This estate is understood to be comprised of four parts: his resurrection, his ascension, his session, and his return.

### Resurrection

The Nicene Creed begins this transition to Christ's estate of exaltation by quoting directly from 1 Corinthians 15:4, which says, "and the third day he rose again according to the scriptures."

John Calvin wrote in the *Institutes of the Christian Religion* that without the resurrection of Christ — without this beginning part of his exaltation — all that has previously been said of Christ's estate of humiliation would be defective. For seeing that in the cross, death, and burial of Christ, nothing but weakness appears, faith must go beyond all these in order that it may be provided with full strength.

The Apostle Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15:14–19 that if Christ is not risen, then our preaching is empty and your faith is also empty, and we are of all men most to be pitied. But praise be to our God that Paul declares in verse 20:

> *"But now Christ is risen from the dead, and has become the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep."* (1 Corinthians 15:20)

So Christ's estate of exaltation begins with his resurrection. It is here that we see the fulfillment of Psalm 16:9–10 in the person of Christ:

> *"Therefore my heart is glad, and my glory rejoices; my soul also will rest in hope. For you will not leave my soul in Sheol, nor will you allow your Holy One to see corruption."* (Psalm 16:9–10)

Our Lord, by his resurrection, confirms that he is who he says he is — the only begotten Son of God, begotten of the Father before all worlds, God of God, light of light, very God of very God — because it is God alone who has power over death.

> *"I lay down my life that I may take it again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again. This command I have received from my Father."* (John 10:17–18)

### Ascension

The Creed continues with this ascent into Christ's exaltation by stating that Christ "ascended into heaven." While we might not think much about this short phrase, the implication of denying the bodily ascension of Christ into heaven is grave — if you deny it, your soul is in a very dangerous place.

We affirm what the Nicene Creed says and what Scripture says. It is the Lord Jesus Christ — the God-man, truly God, truly man — who ascended in both body and soul into heaven to be seated at the right hand of the Father. We must remember that if Christ has not ascended as the God-man, then he cannot be seated at the right hand of the Father making intercession for his people as our federal head and mediator of the covenant of grace. His exaltation must include his bodily ascension because he must be our great High Priest.

> *"Now when he had spoken these things, while they watched, he was taken up, and a cloud received him out of their sight. And while they looked steadfastly toward heaven as he went up, behold, two men stood by them in white apparel, who also said, 'Men of Galilee, why do you stand gazing up into heaven? This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will so come in like manner as you saw him go into heaven.'"* (Acts 1:9–11)

### Session

It was for the purpose of being further exalted that, as the Creed affirms, upon his ascension Christ now "sits on the right hand of the Father."

> *"...which he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places, far above all principality and power and might and dominion, and every name that is named, not only in this age but also in that which is to come. And he put all things under his feet and gave him to be head over all things to the church, which is his body, the fullness of him who fills all in all."* (Ephesians 1:20–23)

As the God-man, the Lord is exalted to the highest place of honor and glory as a reward for his work for us men and for our salvation.

> *"The LORD said to my Lord, 'Sit at my right hand, till I make your enemies your footstool.' The LORD shall send the rod of your strength out of Zion. Rule in the midst of your enemies."* (Psalm 110:1–2)

Before his ascension and session, Jesus declared to his church:

> *"All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I have commanded you; and lo, I am with you always, even to the end of the age."* (Matthew 28:18–20)

I don't know about you, but for myself in thinking about Christ's session — something I often forget — Christ is not seated in heaven *waiting* to rule his church. Christ is presently *ruling and reigning* over his church. He is gathering her, defending her, subduing her enemies, furnishing her with ministers, and giving his people gifts and graces, and making intercession for her. That is not a king waiting to rule. That is a king — our Lord, the Lord Jesus Christ — ruling and reigning over his church right now.

> *"He who descended is also the one who ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things. And he himself gave some to be apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, and some pastors and teachers, for the equipping of the saints for the work of the ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ."* (Ephesians 4:10–12)

To know that in every moment — whether great or small, whether spiritual or bodily, whether in life or in death — we have one who stands as our head before the Father, who ever lives and pleads for his people, is of great comfort to all those who are under the headship of Christ.

### Return

The Creed concludes this section on Christ's exaltation with this glorious truth and promise: "and he shall come again with glory to judge the living and the dead, whose kingdom shall have no end."

This is the promise to which we cling. This is the highest and greatest exaltation of Christ — when he returns to gather his bride to himself and bring her to glory evermore.

> *"And I heard, as it were, the voice of a great multitude, as the sound of many waters and as the sound of mighty thunderings, saying, 'Hallelujah! For the Lord God Omnipotent reigns! Let us be glad and rejoice and give him glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his wife has made herself ready.' And to her it was granted to be arrayed in fine linen, clean and bright, for the fine linen is the righteous acts of the saints. Then he said to me, 'Write: Blessed are those who are called to the marriage supper of the Lamb!' And he said to me, 'These are the true sayings of God.'"* (Revelation 19:6–9)

Christ's exaltation brings us hope.

## Conclusion

So brothers and sisters, as we started this lesson, our chief theological task is not to reinvent, not to be relevant, but to remember. We must remember this Lord — the Lord Jesus Christ, the only begotten Son of God, begotten of the Father before all worlds, God of God, light of light, very God of very God — who for us men and for our salvation came down from heaven and was incarnate by the Holy Spirit of the Virgin Mary and was made man. And was crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate. He suffered and was buried, and the third day he rose again according to the scriptures, and ascended into heaven, and sits on the right hand of the Father. And he shall come again with glory to judge the living and the dead, whose kingdom shall have no end.

This is what we believe. This is what we confess. And this is what the Nicene Creed affirms. These are the truths we proclaim in all of life, to the glory of God.
